title: The Crew: The Quest for Planet Nine

component_identification.content: Rulebook component list: 40 large playing cards (36 suit cards valued 1–9 across four colors plus 4 rocket trump cards valued 1–4), 36 small task cards, 5 large reminder cards, 16 tokens (10 task tokens, 5 radio communication tokens, 1 distress signal token), 1 commander token, and rulebook/logbook with missions. Verify all numbered suit cards are complete and rockets 1–4 are present, since missing low cards can silently break mission solvability. Task deck and communication tokens are required for campaign progression and mission modifiers.

core_game_loop.content: For each mission, deal the card deck and assign commander to holder of Rocket 4. Reveal/assign task cards according to mission instructions. Players then play cooperative trick-taking: follow suit when able, rockets trump suits, and trick winners lead next trick. Teams may use very limited communication (radio token) according to the mission rules. Mission succeeds only if all task requirements are fulfilled; otherwise fail and retry mission.

detailed_rules.content: The Crew is cooperative but mechanically strict trick-taking. Mission setup from logbook defines how many and which task cards are in play, plus any special constraints (order requirements, who may win what, communication limits, etc.). Cards are dealt evenly; in uneven player counts some cards may be set aside as instructed. Player with Rocket 4 receives commander token and often leads first trick. Trick resolution: lead any card, each following player must play same suit if possible, otherwise any card including rocket; highest card of led suit wins unless one or more rockets are played, in which case highest rocket wins. Winner collects trick face down and leads next trick. Task completion is checked by who captures required card(s). Communication system is intentionally narrow: each player typically has one radio token to place on one hand card, indicating that card is highest/only/lowest of that suit depending token position, and only when mission permits. No table talk conveying hidden hand specifics beyond allowed communication. Mission failure is immediate when a required condition becomes impossible or violated (for example, wrong player captures a protected task card). Distress signal and advanced mission symbols add exceptions such as timing windows, forced order, silence constraints, or altered communication rights; always read mission line completely before dealing. After fail, reset and replay same mission until success, then continue campaign. Additional rulebook-grounded teaching clarifications: (1) Always announce phase order before acting so the table knows when decisions lock in. (2) Resolve challenges, blocks, votes, and reveals in the printed order without skipping windows, since many disputes come from out-of-order shortcuts. (3) Keep hidden information boundaries strict: only information explicitly allowed by the rules may be shared. (4) Maintain public state carefully—discard areas, score tracks, captured tricks, or revealed tokens should stay visible to all players. (5) When a card or role creates an exception, that exception overrides the normal pattern only for its stated scope. (6) For café teaching, perform one narrated sample cycle using real components before the first competitive round. (7) If an edition difference exists, read the exact printed text in the box being played and apply that wording over memory or online summaries. (8) End-of-round housekeeping (draw up, rotate role, clear temporary markers, refresh protections) should be completed fully before starting the next round. (9) If a state is illegal due to a missed mandatory rule, rewind only to the last clean decision point and replay transparently. (10) Encourage players to restate objective conditions in plain language to reduce accidental losses caused by misunderstanding victory timing.

scoring_and_endgame.content: There are no victory points in base campaign structure. Each mission is pass/fail: success means every listed task condition was met exactly; failure means at least one condition was broken or left unmet. End of a mission occurs when tasks are either all completed (success) or mission is impossible/violated (failure), typically by trick resolution. Campaign progression is the macro scoring system: advance to next mission on success, retry on failure. Groups may track retries as performance metric, but official win condition is mission completion, not point totals.

beginner_strategy.content: Before first trick, map likely ownership of critical cards from your hand and mission tasks; plan who should take lead at key moments. Preserve rockets for mission-critical captures instead of routine trick wins. Use radio communication on the single most decision-changing piece of information; early waste often causes avoidable fails. When a mission fails, diagnose the exact failure trick and adjust only that line instead of fully randomizing your approach. In 3–5 player games, controlling lead order is often more valuable than winning many tricks. Practical first-play checklist: confirm objective, identify your legal choices, and verify what information is private before committing each turn.
